speaker: Unknown
en: You still need humans in the loop.
zh: 你仍然需要人在回路中。

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't surrender to the machine.
zh: 不要向机器投降。

speaker: Unknown
en: We can use the machines, but don't cognitively surrender.
zh: 我们可以使用机器，但不要在认知上投降。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because it's so easy to build, the things that stand out are the things that are really well thought through.
zh: 因为它太容易构建了，所以脱颖而出的东西是那些真正经过深思熟虑的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today in the AI world, I can just make a prompt, and all of a sudden, it gets spit out.
zh: 如今在 AI 世界里，我可以直接做一个提示词，突然间，它就被吐出来了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're building on a really crusty foundation.
zh: 你正在一个非常脆弱的基础上构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're getting short-term gain for very, very long-term laws.
zh: 你正在获得短期的收益，却是为了非常、非常长期的代价。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're going to build a real company, it can't be throwaway.
zh: 如果你要建立一家真正的公司，它不能是可丢弃的。

speaker: Unknown
en: When your colleague's Hermann Hauser, then ask him how he decides what is worth building.
zh: 当你的同事是 Hermann Hauser 时，然后问他如何决定什么是值得构建的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always start from pain.
zh: 我总是从痛点开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are there new technologies to solve that pain?
zh: 有没有新技术来解决那个痛点？

speaker: Unknown
en: Bring innovation in, revolution in, then redefine the space.
zh: 引入创新，引入革命，然后重新定义这个领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the threshold?
zh: 阈值是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's a sign of, "Okay, this isn't big enough."
zh: 有什么迹象表明，“好吧，这个不够大。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, the iPod wasn't big enough.
zh: 噢，iPod 不够大。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took three generations of the iPod before it became successful.
zh: 它花了三代 iPod 才变得成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: You got to fail a few times until you find your way.
zh: 你得失败几次才能找到你的路。

speaker: Unknown
en: You are so into marketing that piece of building that I think a lot of builders don't think about at all.
zh: 你太热衷于推销那部分构建环节了，我认为很多构建者根本不考虑那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: The technology's in service of the customer, not we're going to jam the technology down the customer's throat.
zh: 技术是为客户服务的，而不是我们要把技术硬塞给客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: A customer only sees what they see through the lens of marketing.
zh: 客户只看到他们透过营销镜头看到的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: You often come back to the value of storytelling for product builders.
zh: 你经常回到讲故事对产品构建者的价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Too many times when we're technology-led, we talk about the what.
zh: 太多次当我们由技术驱动时，我们谈论是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't talk about the why.
zh: 我们不谈论为什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: The why is with storytelling.
zh: “为什么”在于讲故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: When I watched Steve, he was honing the story of the iPhone every day.
zh: 当我看 Steve 的时候，他每天都在打磨 iPhone 的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when you saw him come on stage, it was just because he had done it a hundred thousand times.
zh: 所以当你看到他上台时，那是因为他已经做了十万次了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Tony Fadell.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Tony Fadell。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tony doesn't know this, but ever since I started this podcast, he's been near the top of my wishlist of people that I've dreamed to have on this podcast.
zh: Tony 不知道这一点，但自从我开始这个播客以来，他一直是我梦寐以求想邀请上这个节目的人的愿望名单的前几名。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's because Tony is the epitome of what most people listening to this podcast want to become.
zh: 那是因为 Tony 是大多数听这个播客的人想成为的人的缩影。

speaker: Unknown
en: He co-created some of the most innovative and beautiful and popular products in history.
zh: 他共同创造了一些历史上最具创新性、最美丽和最受欢迎的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: The iPod, the iPhone, the Nest Thermostat.
zh: iPod、iPhone、Nest 恒温器。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's also famous for being part of the legendary team at General Magic.
zh: 他也因是 General Magic 传奇团队的一员而闻名。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's co-authored over 300 patents.
zh: 他共同撰写了 300 多项专利。

speaker: Unknown
en: He also wrote one of the most important and valuable and inspiring books for builders called Build.
zh: 他还为构建者撰写了一本最重要、最有价值和最具启发性的书之一，名为 Build。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tony is currently an active investor and advisor to deep-tech startups with his team at the Build Collective.
zh: Tony 目前是 Build Collective 团队中深度科技初创公司的活跃投资者和顾问。

speaker: Unknown
en: He was recently named the Inaugural Designer in Residence at the MIT Morning Academy of Design.
zh: 他最近被任命为 MIT Morning Academy of Design 的首位驻校设计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so much gold in this episode.
zh: 这一集里有太多金子了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I could go on and on.
zh: 我可以滔滔不绝地讲。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to leave it there.
zh: 我就说到这儿。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lennysproductpass.com for a free year of some of the hottest and most well-crafted AI products in the world, available exclusively to Lenny's Newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在我们进入正题之前，别忘了去看看 Lennysproductpass.com，免费获得一些世界上最热门、最精心制作的 AI 产品，专门提供给 Lenny's Newsletter 的订阅者。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Tony Fadell.
zh: 有了这个，我为您带来 Tony Fadell。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tony, thank you so much for being here, and welcome to the podcast.
zh: Tony，非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks for having me.
zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: I appreciate it.
zh: 我很感激。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a bazillion questions I want to ask you.
zh: 我有无数个问题想问你。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like I could feel four hours of conversation with all the things that I want to get out of your head.
zh: 我觉得我可以和你聊四个小时，把我想从你脑子里掏出来的所有东西都掏出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to start with the BlackBerry.
zh: 我想从 BlackBerry 开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was just watching the Blackberry movie recently, and it's kind of this journey of the BlackBerry founders and their story.
zh: 我最近刚看了 BlackBerry 电影，它是 BlackBerry 创始人及其故事的一段旅程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then at the end, they're like, "Oh, and this iPhone thing launched."
zh: 然后在最后，他们就像，“噢，这个 iPhone 东西发布了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're like, "No, this is dumb.
zh: 他们就像，“不，这太蠢了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like no keyboard.
zh: 就像没有键盘。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not serious.
zh: 这不严肃。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can't do anything with it."
zh: 用它做不了任何事。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I've always wondered, just being on the other side of this, being within Apple building the iPhone, how much did you guys actually doubt that, "Okay, maybe they have something, maybe we need to add a keyboard."
zh: 我一直很好奇，站在另一边，身处苹果内部构建 iPhone，你们当时到底有多大程度怀疑，“好吧，也许他们有东西，也许我们需要添加一个键盘。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It was the most heated conversation and it dragged out the longest.
zh: 这是最激烈的争论，也是拖得最久的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There was one way of looking at the BlackBerry, which was that is the market we want to go after or we want to win.
zh: 有一种看待 BlackBerry 的方式是，那是我们要瞄准的市场或者我们要赢下的市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's the other side, the flip side of that argument, which is only 1% or 2% of mobile phone users at the time had a BlackBerry, knew what a BlackBerry was.
zh: 然后另一面，也就是那个论点的反面，是当时只有 1% 或 2% 的手机用户拥有 BlackBerry，知道 BlackBerry 是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what about the other 98% of the people?
zh: 那么其他的 98% 的人呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: What would they want?
zh: 他们会想要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What would they need?
zh: 他们会需要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why are we going to go after winning this very loyal and incredibly passionate user base and try to pull them away from something?
zh: 我们为什么要去赢取这个非常忠诚且充满热情的用户群，并试图把他们从某种东西上拉走呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there was this basically head-to-head competition between a display keyboard or virtual keyboard and a physical keyboard.
zh: 所以这基本上就是显示屏键盘或虚拟键盘与物理键盘之间的正面交锋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I had been doing virtual keyboards for a while since General Magic in the '90s, and I knew what handwriting was and keyboards were like on these touchscreens and... but I was only doing it on a... and I was writing software and calibrating them and trying to make them work with a single-touch display, resistive or what have you.
zh: 我从 90 年代的 General Magic 开始就做虚拟键盘有一段时间了，我知道手写和键盘在这些触摸屏上是什么样的……但我只是在……上做，我在编写软件并校准它们，试图让它们与单点触摸显示屏配合工作，不管是电阻屏还是别的什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I knew what the limitations were of those kinds of things.
zh: 所以我知道这类东西的局限性是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I was like, "Hmm, this is really going to be difficult."
zh: 所以我想，“嗯，这真的很难。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had... Multitouch just was on a big ping-pong table.
zh: 而我们有……Multitouch 就在一张大大的乒乓球桌上。

speaker: Unknown
en: It hadn't been scaled down, so it wasn't like something in a consumptive form where you could really do user test with it.
zh: 它还没有被缩小，所以它不像是一个消费品形态的东西，你真的可以拿它做用户测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we set out a set of tests like, "Okay, how fast can I type this text?
zh: 所以我们要进行一系列测试，比如，“好吧，我打这段文字能有多快？

speaker: Unknown
en: How fast can I do this on a hardware keyboard?
zh: 我在硬件键盘上做这个能有多快？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then how can we do this on the virtual one with multitouch?"
zh: 那么我们在多点触摸的虚拟键盘上做这个能有多快？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was a hardware-software integration challenge of how we could get this to work.
zh: 这是一个软硬件集成的挑战，关于我们要如何让它工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we were going back and forth and back and forth, "Oh, that doesn't quite work in the software.
zh: 所以我们反复来回，“噢，这在软件里不太行。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, we need to change this in the hardware."
zh: 噢，我们需要在硬件里改这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this was over a set of months would, "Okay, the hardware keyboards here," and depends on how... how much you've been using it, but there's this margin error, and we could really understand it.
zh: 所以这是在几个月的时间里，“好的，硬件键盘在这里，”这取决于你……你用了多少，但这有一个误差范围，我们真的能理解它。

speaker: Unknown
en: This, over time, was started way down here, and it started to get it, and it got a little faster and a little faster and a little faster.
zh: 这，随着时间推移，是从这里开始的，然后开始起步，变得快一点，再快一点，再快一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And how many errors, not just how fast, but how many errors, and how do you correct the errors, and all of those things.
zh: 以及有多少错误，不仅是多快，而是有多少错误，你如何纠正错误，以及所有这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And at the end of the day, I was able to convince myself it wasn't going to be a hardware issue, and I was convinced at some point that we were good enough.
zh: 最终，我能够说服自己这不是一个硬件问题，我在某个点确信我们已经足够好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Were we as good as a hardware keyboard?
zh: 我们和硬件键盘一样好吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: No.
zh: 不。

speaker: Unknown
en: But were we good enough?
zh: 但我们足够好吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then other people came to that conclusion, but at the same time, there were other people who were adamant that the hardware keyboard has to be there, and they were unrelenting.
zh: 然后其他人也得出了这个结论，但同时，也有其他人坚持认为硬件键盘必须存在，并且他们不妥协。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it came down to...
zh: 所以归结为……

speaker: Unknown
en: So this was a classic, like I say in Build, data versus opinion-based decision.
zh: 所以这是一个经典的，就像我在《Build》里说的，数据与基于意见的决策。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you think about it, you had data that said there was pros and cons on both sides.
zh: 如果你想想看，你有数据表明双方都有优缺点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what happened was the data was not clear that we should choose one over the other.
zh: 发生的情况是，数据并不清楚我们应该选择其中一个而不是另一个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Steve said, "We are going this way."
zh: 然后史蒂夫说，“我们要走这条路。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Enough other people kind of said, "Yeah, that seems like that's the right thing to do.
zh: 足够多的其他人说，“是的，这似乎是正确的事情要做。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to get close enough to get there."
zh: 我们会足够接近以达成目标。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then other people were like, " No, my opinion is this."
zh: 然后其他人说，“不，我的意见是这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And guess who wins at the end of the day?
zh: 猜猜最后谁赢了？

speaker: Unknown
en: Steve Jobs' opinion does.
zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯的意见赢了。

speaker: Lenny
en: Steve Jobs.
zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he was like, "If you're not going to get on board, get out of this room, and you can go work on another project, but you're not going to work on this one."
zh: 他就像，“如果你不打算上船，就滚出这个房间，你可以去另一个项目工作，但你不能在这个项目上工作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by our season's presenting sponsor, WorkOS.
zh: 这一集由我们本季度的主赞助商 WorkOS 赞助。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, Replit, Sierra, Clay, and hundreds of other winning companies all have in common?
zh: OpenAI、Anthropic、Cursor、Vercel、Replit、Sierra、Clay 以及数百家其他获胜的公司有什么共同点？

speaker: Unknown
en: They are all powered by WorkOS.
zh: 它们都由 WorkOS 驱动。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're building a product for the enterprise, you've felt the pain of integrating single sign-on, SCIM, RBAC, audit logs, and other features required by large companies.
zh: 如果你正在为企业构建产品，你已经感受到了集成单点登录、SCIM、RBAC、审计日志以及大公司所需的其他功能的痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS turns those deal blockers into drop-in APIs with a modern developer platform built specifically for B2B SaaS.
zh: WorkOS 利用专为 B2B SaaS 构建的现代化开发平台，将这些交易障碍变成了插入式 API。

speaker: Unknown
en: Literally every startup that I'm an investor in that starts to expand upmarket ends up working with WorkOS, and that's because they are the best.
zh: 实际上，我投资的每一家开始向高端市场扩张的初创公司最终都与 WorkOS 合作，因为他们是最棒的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you are a seed-stage startup trying to land your first enterprise customer or a unicorn expanding globally, WorkOS is the fastest path to becoming enterprise-ready and unblocking growth.
zh: 无论你是试图达成第一家企业客户的种子阶段初创公司，还是正在全球扩张的独角兽，WorkOS 是成为企业就绪并解除增长阻碍的最快途径。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's essentially Stripe for enterprise features.
zh: 它本质上就是用于企业功能的 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit workos.com to get started, or just hit up their Slack, where they have actual engineers waiting to answer your questions.
zh: 访问 workos.com 开始使用，或者直接联系他们的 Slack，那里有真正的工程师在等待回答你的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS allows you to build faster with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许你通过令人愉悦的 API、全面的文档和流畅的开发者体验更快地构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise-ready today.
zh: 访问 workos.com 让你的应用在今天变得企业就绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: One direction I want to get into here is this idea that you talk a lot about micromanaging being actually really important and powerful.
zh: 我想在这里深入探讨的一个方向是，你谈到很多关于微观管理实际上非常重要和强大的这个观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's this image that's floating around Twitter that I don't know if you've seen.
zh: 所以有一张在 Twitter 上流传的图片，我不知道你有没有看过。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll show it on the screen as I'm describing it, and hopefully you can visualize it.
zh: 在我描述的时候我会把它展示在屏幕上，希望你能想象出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a functional system versus a dysfunctional system.
zh: 这是一个功能正常的系统与功能失调的系统的对比。

speaker: Unknown
en: Basically, a company that's doing great versus not great.
zh: 基本上，就是一个做得很好的公司与一个做得不好的公司的对比。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's this chart that goes up to more functional that is basically unkind truth, and then another unkind truth, another unkind truth creates more functional systems.
zh: 有一个图表向上延伸到更具功能性，基本上就是不友善的真相，然后又一个不友善的真相，又一个不友善的真相创造了更具功能性的系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: Kind lie followed by kind lie, followed by kind lie, leads to dysfunctional systems.
zh: 善意的谎言，接着善意的谎言，再接着善意的谎言，导致功能失调的系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something Steve Jobs is very famous for.
zh: 这是史蒂夫·乔布斯非常有名的一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: In your book, you talk about mission-driven assholes are actually really... You want a mission-driven asshole.
zh: 在你的书中，你谈到以使命驱动的混蛋实际上真的很……你想要一个以使命驱动的混蛋。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's certain types of assholes that are great.
zh: 有某些类型的混蛋是很棒的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about just the importance of somebody being very direct in what it takes to build great products.
zh: 谈谈某人非常直接的重要性，这对构建伟大的产品意味着什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you're doing a 1.0 of anything, when you're doing... if you're doing anything that matters, and it's a 1.0, and it's a new category, or it's a new device the world hasn't seen before, you have very few analogs that you can use to make data-driven decisions.
zh: 当你在做任何事的 1.0 版本时，当你在做……如果你在做任何重要的事情，而且它是 1.0 版本，它是一个新品类，或者是世界上以前没见过的新设备，你可以用来做出数据驱动决策的类比非常少。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if most of your decisions are going to be opinion-based decisions for a 1.0, you have to have one or two or a very, very small set of people who are charged with making the opinion-based decisions and can actually get you from point A to... from a white paper or a white... bank sheet of white paper or whiteboard to an actual 1.0 spec.
zh: 所以如果你大部分的决策都将是基于意见的决策，对于一个 1.0 版本来说，你必须有一两个或非常非常少的一组人，他们负责做出基于意见的决策，并且实际上能让你从 A 点到……从一份白皮书或白……一叠白纸或白板到一个实际的 1.0 规格。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because if you try to do data-driven decisions all the way along, you're either not doing a differentiated product because you're taking data from another thing, or you're just getting just bullshit data, right.
zh: 因为如果你一路尝试做数据驱动的决策，你要么不是在做差异化的产品，因为你在从其他东西获取数据，要么你只是在得到废话数据，对吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you're going to have to figure out how to get opinion-based decisions to happen, and that means you have to have, for lack of a better word, taste makers.
zh: 所以你必须弄清楚如何让基于意见的决策发生，这意味着你必须拥有，打个比方说，潮流制造者。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is what we are doing.
zh: 这就是我们正在做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are the person or the team who is going to make those opinion-based decisions.
zh: 我们就是要做出那些基于意见的决策的人或团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: Of course, some people aren't going to like it, and it's going to be like, "I'm sorry, this is a benevolent dictatorship.
zh: 当然，有些人不会喜欢它，它会像是，“对不起，这是一个仁慈的独裁。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is what's going to happen, and this is the vision."
zh: 这就是将要发生的事情，这就是愿景。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And we don't know what we don't know until we ship it and we get opinions from the users.
zh: 在我们发布它并从用户那里获得意见之前，我们不知道我们不知道的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it's very different when you do this in a B2B context versus a B2C context.
zh: 现在在 B2B 背景下做这件事与在 B2C 背景下做这件事是非常不同的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the hardest environment to work with it when you have these opinion-based distinctions is in a B2C context because you have to see these decisions in the full light and you don't... a consumer does.
zh: 所以当你有这些基于意见的区别时，最难工作的环境是在 B2C 背景下，因为你必须在完全的光线下看到这些决策，而你不会……消费者会。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have to see it from the marketing, from how they discover it in the marketing, the key feature sets, the ability to use the product, all of these different things for them to actually come up with an opinion of what they like, and what they don't like and being able to critique it.
zh: 他们必须从营销、从他们如何在营销中发现它、关键功能集、使用产品的能力，所有这些不同的事情来看待它，以便他们实际上能够得出他们喜欢什么、不喜欢的意见，并且能够批评它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you don't... And if you're doing a 1.0 and the world hasn't seen, you're not going to get that from consumers ever.
zh: 如果你不……如果你在做 1.0 版本而世界以前没见过，你永远不会从消费者那里得到那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to ship it, and you have to build the entire kind of ecosystem so those consumers see it in the fullness, so that when they do the evaluation, and they spend their own money, then you're getting real feedback.
zh: 你必须发布它，并且你必须构建整个这种生态系统，以便那些消费者能够完整地看到它，这样当他们进行评估时，他们花了他们自己的钱，然后你就得到了真正的反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: When it comes to consumer... atoms-based products with services or not, whatever it is, you have to build the entire thing and visualize the entire thing to be able to make those opinion-based decisions.
zh: 当涉及到消费者……基于原子的产品，无论有没有服务，不管它是什么，你必须构建整个东西并把整个东西可视化，以便能够做出那些基于意见的决策。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you need a small team who's looking at the marketing angles, the engineering angles, the sales angles, all those different things to go, "Okay, that's the way we're going."
zh: 所以你需要一个小团队来关注营销角度、工程角度、销售角度，所有那些不同的事情，然后说，“好吧，这就是我们要走的路。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's only so many ways you can do it because the rest of the team doesn't see this either.
zh: 只有这么多方法可以做到，因为团队的其他人员也看不到这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you have to be very articulate about how... what that opinion-based decision is made for, why, how it might affect market, how you do it, and make sure the team understands it.
zh: 所以你必须非常清楚地表达如何……做出那个基于意见的决策是为了什么，为什么，它如何可能影响市场，你如何做，并确保团队理解它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, if the team is just fully against it, well then maybe it has to be unkind, but hopefully if you've done a good job and you really have an informed gut and you can articulate that, you can get everybody moving in the same direction even if it is... And it's going to be risky.
zh: 现在，如果团队完全反对它，那好吧，也许它必须是不友善的，但希望如果你做得很好，并且你真的有一个消息灵通的直觉，并且你能清楚地表达它，你可以让每个人都朝同一个方向前进，即使它是……而且这将是冒险的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're right.
zh: 你是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You got to know that you got to take risk because most people in those other functions, they don't want to take any risks.
zh: 你必须知道你必须承担风险，因为在那些其他职能中的大多数人，他们不想承担任何风险。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there's someone who's got to be the target, right.
zh: 所以必须有一个人成为靶子，对吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: And today, in many contexts, people go and hire consultants, right.
zh: 今天，在许多背景下，人们去聘请顾问，对吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: They go and hire, "Okay, we're going to do a user study, or we're going to do all this."
zh: 他们去聘请，“好吧，我们要做一个用户研究，或者我们要做所有这些。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And they don't have the user studies and have the full context like I was just describing.
zh: 而且他们没有像我刚才描述的那样拥有完整背景的用户研究。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't go buy the product, and they don't what have you and then they get data, and that's because the leader or whoever's in there, board maybe, it's like, "We need data to make sure this 1.0 is going to be a success."
zh: 他们不去购买产品，他们也不拥有什么，然后他们得到数据，这是因为领导者或在那里的任何人，可能是董事会，这就像是，“我们需要数据来确保这个 1.0 版本会成功。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I saw this so many times at all these major corporations, so they're just kind of covering their with bullshit data and not really doing the hard work of saying, "I'm going to make this decision and we are going to select this, and yes, I might be wrong or we as the opinion based decision makers are wrong and we will correct it later and we'll take the heat for that."
zh: 我在所有这些大公司看到过很多次这种情况，所以他们只是在用废话数据掩盖他们的并且没有真正做艰苦的工作，说，“我要做出这个决策，我们要选择这个，是的，我可能是错的，或者我们作为基于意见的决策制定者错了，我们稍后会纠正它，我们将为此承担责难。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's the... a great product manager or a great person who's leading this thing has to understand that that's what they have to do if they're really doing something innovative.
zh: 所以这就是……一个伟大的产品经理或一个领导这件事的伟大人物必须理解这就是他们必须做的，如果他们真的在做创新的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I'm hearing here is just the power, especially in a consumer product, of a singular vision of a singular leader that drives it, that is... basically relies on their instinct and their taste and their experience.
zh: 所以我在这里听到的是，尤其是在消费品中，单一领导者的单一愿景的力量，驱动着它，基本上是依靠他们的直觉、他们的品味和他们的经验。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a lot of...
zh: 而且很多……

speaker: Unknown
en: But again, a lot of informed judgment from all the experts around asking questions, refining, prototyping these kinds of things to then make a decision.
zh: 但同样，来自周围所有专家的大量经过深思熟虑的判断，提出问题、完善、制作这些东西的原型，然后做出决策。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's not just like, I woke up one morning, and this is... now it's we're going this direction.
zh: 所以这不仅仅是，我有一天早上醒来，这就是……现在我们要走这个方向。

speaker: Unknown

en: I want to come back to the idea of micromanaging, which a lot of people... to a lot of people that word is bad and don't be a micromanager.

en: Just speak to that, what people you think miss about the importance and power of micromanaging as a leader.

en: We've heard the term sweat the details.

speaker: Unknown
en: It's micromanagement of certain details, and then there's the kind of hands-off of other details.
zh: 这是对某些细节的微观管理，然后对其他细节则是那种放手不管。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to really understand the blend of which things really matter, which things don't.
zh: 你必须真正理解这种混合，即哪些事情真正重要，哪些不重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: When I was early on my career, I thought everything mattered, and I drove everybody nuts, drove myself nuts, the people hated...
zh: 当我职业生涯早期时，我认为每件事都重要，我把所有人都逼疯了，也把自己逼疯了，人们讨厌……

speaker: Unknown
en: And it became my... became like, everybody's got to do it the way I would do it.
zh: 这变成了我的……变得像是，每个人都必须按我会做的方式去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, no, no, no, no.
zh: 就像是，不，不，不，不。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's only a few key things, mostly for the customer or maybe some certain things from manufacturing or cost or something where it needs to really be very clear or a long-term vision.
zh: 只有少数关键的事情，主要是为了客户，或者是某些来自制造或成本或某些方面的事情，在这些地方需要真正非常清晰，或者是长期愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then you can delegate other things, but certain pieces of it you really need to... And when I mean micromanage, it means micromanage the decision, not necessarily the operations of doing it, making sure you're getting the data like we did with the keyboard on the iPhone to make sure we're getting the right data to help us get the informed gut to make that opinion-based decision.
zh: 但然后你可以把其他事情委派出去，但其中的某些部分你真的需要……而当我所说的微观管理时，是指微观管理决策，而不一定是执行它的操作，要确保你获得数据，就像我们在 iPhone 的键盘上所做的那样，确保我们获得正确的数据来帮助我们获得有依据的直觉，从而做出基于该观点的决策。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's the micro specification and delivery of those certain data pieces that you need, and maybe it's also to get out of a crisis, or maybe it's a system-level thing where you have to worry about this thing at the low level changing up here and here, and like, "We could do that, but only if they do this and this."
zh: 因此，这就是对你需要的那些特定数据片段的微观规范和交付，也许这也是为了摆脱危机，或者也许这是一个系统级的事情，你必须担心这个低层次的东西在这里和这里产生变化，比如，“我们可以做那个，但只有当他们做这个和这个的时候。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "Okay, guys, we're going to fix all of those things at once."
zh: 就像是，“好的，伙计们，我们要一次修复所有那些东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have to micromanage it because everybody wants to find the excuses why they can't do that, or they can't do that.

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have to ask why a lot.
zh: 所以你必须经常问为什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it just...
zh: 所以这只是……

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's all in service of some really key detail that needs to get delivered or some innovation that needs to be delivered.
zh: 但这都是为了服务于某些真正需要交付的关键细节，或者某些需要交付的创新。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like, like I said, the keyboard, we had to do the hardware, we had to do the software, we had to do the filtering.
zh: 就像我刚才说的，键盘，我们必须做硬件，我们必须做软件，我们必须做过滤。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had to do how it was... how the graphics were done on the screen.
zh: 我们必须做它是如何……图形是如何在屏幕上完成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you had all of these layers that had to keep constantly changing and adjusting.
zh: 所以你有所有这些层必须不断变化和调整。

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes you have to micromanage that because there's just too many variables and someone has to be the orchestrator of this huge orchestra of many different components to kind of make it all come together and be harmonious.
zh: 有时你必须微观管理它，因为有太多的变量，必须有一个人成为这个由许多不同组件组成的巨大管弦乐队的指挥，以使所有这些东西聚集在一起并和谐。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the way you give best context is by having sensors properly placed around the home that don't necessarily invade privacy, but allow you to pick up a lot of comings and goings, and who's who in the room, and your voice, and these kinds of things.
zh: 而提供最佳上下文的方式，就是在家里恰当地放置传感器，不一定侵犯隐私，但能让你捕捉到很多进出活动，谁在房间里，以及你的声音，这类事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Audio, I should say, not voice, to be able to give AI's context so that you can have a anywhere assistant that really knows what's going on.
zh: 是音频，我该说，不是语音，以便能够给 AI 上下文，这样你就可以有一个真正知道发生了什么的随处可用的助手。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there a space for someone to launch the new Nest, do you think?
zh: 你觉得有人有空间推出新的 Nest 吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, for sure.
zh: 哦，当然。

speaker: Unknown
en: For sure.
zh: 当然。

speaker: Unknown
en: People keep asking me, "Are we going to get it?"
zh: 人们一直问我，“我们会得到它吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, no, absolutely.
zh: 就像是，不，绝对会。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now's the time if this isn't already happening.
zh: 如果这还没发生的话，现在就是时候了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm actually getting business plans of people who are like, "Hey, is this interesting to you?
zh: 我实际上收到了一些商业计划书，人们就像是，“嘿，这对你来说有趣吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Want to invest in it?
zh: 想要投资吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: You want to come and help us build it?
zh: 你想来帮助我们构建它吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: This is like Nest 2.0."
zh: 这就像是 Nest 2.0。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So they're trying to do stuff like that at Ring.
zh: 所以他们试图在 Ring 做类似的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think very... Not very privacy-focused, but that's what they're trying to do.
zh: 我不认为非常……不是很注重隐私，但这正是他们试图做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was our vision in the beginning, to tell you the truth.
zh: 坦白说，那是我们最初的愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's how we were like, "You're going to need lots of context [inaudible 00:20:22]."
zh: 我们当时就像是，“你会需要很多上下文 [听不清 00:20:22]。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Because we...
zh: 因为我们……

speaker: Unknown
en: Remember, the Nest Learning Thermostat wasn't the Nest AI Thermostat.
zh: 记得，Nest Learning Thermostat 不是 Nest AI Thermostat。

speaker: Unknown
en: It could have been called that, but we couldn't call it that in 2011 because people would have freaked out.
zh: 它本可以叫那个名字，但我们在 2011 年不能那样叫，因为人们会吓坏的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now you would have called it the Nest AI Thermostat, and people would have bought it.
zh: 现在你如果叫它 Nest AI Thermostat，人们会买它的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we knew what AI was, right.
zh: 所以我们知道 AI 是什么，对吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, since 2010, one of the foundational pieces of the company was AI.
zh: 所以，从 2010 年起，公司的基础部分之一就是 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's how we were able to do a lot of the things we were able to do, but it was much, obviously, not LLMs, but smaller stuff.
zh: 这就是为什么我们能够做我们能做的很多事，但它显然不是 LLM，而是更小的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we said, "Okay, we can see this world growing in this AI assistant."
zh: 我们说，“好吧，我们可以看到这个世界在这个 AI 助手中成长。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Then voice assistance came, then there was Alexa and all that stuff that happened later on in 2013, '14, '15.
zh: 然后语音助理来了，接着有了 Alexa 和所有在后来 2013、2014、2015 年发生的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "Okay."
zh: 就像是，“好的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was gen one, but we could see this kind of blossoming, and unfortunately, in the fullness of time, it takes 15 years more from the time Nest launched for that vision to come to fruition, but it is there now.
zh: 那是第一代，但我们能看到这种萌芽，不幸的是，从长远来看，从 Nest 发布到那个愿景实现，又花了 15 年，但它现在已经在那儿了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if Nest was... We pitched it that way.
zh: 如果 Nest 是……我们当时就是这样推销的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were just too early.
zh: 我们只是太早了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me follow this thread around how to know what to build and how you come up with ideas.
zh: 让我顺着这个话题，关于如何知道该构建什么以及如何想出主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I emailed a bunch of people that know you, I asked them what to ask you.
zh: 所以我给一群认识你的人发了邮件，我问他们该问你什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of your colleagues, Hermann Hauser, Hermann?
zh: 你的一个同事，Hermann Hauser，Hermann？

speaker: Unknown
en: Uh-huh.
zh: 嗯哼。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, Hermann Hauser.
zh: 哦，Hermann Hauser。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hermann Hauser.
zh: Hermann Hauser。

speaker: Unknown
en: So his question he wanted me to ask you said, "Ask him how he decides what is worth building."
zh: 所以他想让我问你的问题是，“问他如何决定什么值得构建。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, Hermann's great.
zh: 嗯，Hermann 很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've known Hermann since 1987, probably before most of your listeners were even born.
zh: 我从 1987 年起就认识 Hermann 了，可能在你们大多数听众出生之前。

speaker: Unknown
en: But Hermann... Just for context, Hermann was the creator of Acorn Computer, which was the Apple II of the UK back in the '70s.
zh: 但 Hermann……只是作为背景，Hermann 是 Acorn Computer 的创造者，那是 70 年代英国的 Apple II。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then he created with his team the ARM processor.
zh: 然后他和他的团队创造了 ARM 处理器。

speaker: Unknown
en: So Acorn RISC...
zh: 所以 Acorn RISC……

speaker: Unknown
en: So ARM means Acorn RISC Machine from Acorn Computer.
zh: 所以 ARM 意味着来自 Acorn Computer 的 Acorn RISC Machine。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so he was one of... one of the founders of ARM.
zh: 所以他是……ARM 的创始人之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I was talking about processor.
zh: 所以我刚才在谈论处理器。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anyway, so that's... so how do... so Hermann and I go way, way back.
zh: 总之，这……所以 Hermann 和我认识很久很久了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the thing is, how do you solve or figure out what is worthy to be built and what's not?
zh: 问题是，你如何解决或弄清楚什么值得构建，什么不值得？

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first thing is I start from pain.
zh: 所以首先，我从痛点开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some people start from other directions.
zh: 有些人从其他方向开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always start from pain.
zh: 我总是从痛点开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's what I learned is what are people's pain right now, or you can see it on the horizon, they're going to have pain, and not too far away, but how do you solve for that pain?
zh: 我学到的是，现在人们的痛点是什么，或者你能预见在不久的将来他们会有痛点，然后你如何解决那个痛点？

speaker: Unknown
en: And typically those pains were because when those products were created, either it was unintentional consequence or it was a limitation of the technology at the time it was created, and it kind of just... it evolved, but it never revolutionized itself.
zh: 通常这些痛点是因为当那些产品被创造时，要么是无意的后果，要么是当时技术的局限，它只是……它演变了，但没有自我革命。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it just evolved, and that same pain kind of was it, but it gave you enough of a painkiller for the other problem that having this new pain was worth it.
zh: 所以它只是演变了，同样的痛点一直存在，但它为另一个问题提供了足够的止痛药，使得忍受这个新痛点是值得的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I always kind of start with, "Okay, where's our current pain, and are there new technologies to solve that pain?"
zh: 所以我总是从，“好吧，我们现在的痛点在哪里，是否有新技术来解决那个痛点？”开始

speaker: Unknown
en: And like in the thermostat case, Nest, was we could use AI to learn so it can learn when you're there, when you're away, what temperatures you're like, so you don't have to program it, so you can save energy.
zh: 就像温控器这个案例，Nest，我们可以使用 AI 来学习，所以它可以学习你什么时候在家，什么时候离家，你喜欢什么温度，这样你就不必给它编程，这样你就可以节省能源。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the big pain was being either comfortable or saving money because 50% of your energy bill was in this heating and cooling unit that you hated the interface, you didn't know what it was, you just paid the bill.
zh: 所以最大的痛点在于要么舒适要么省钱，因为你 50% 的能源账单都花在这个供暖和制冷设备上，你讨厌那个界面，你不知道它是什么，你只是支付账单。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it started from that pain and said, "Okay, well, programmable thermostats weren't innovative, but they weren't used."
zh: 所以它始于那个痛点，并说，“好吧，可编程温控器并不创新，但它们没有被使用。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone... A lot of them had it because the energy company would give you rebates for it, but no one knew how to use it because it was arcane.
zh: 每个人……很多人都有它，因为能源公司会为此给你回扣，但没人知道如何使用它，因为它很晦涩。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was-
zh: 它是-

speaker: Unknown
en: ... give you rebates for it, but no one knew how to use it because it was RK and it was programming a VCR.
zh: ……给你回扣，但没人知道如何使用它，因为它是 RK 而且就像在给录像机编程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what I said was, "Oh, wait a second, what if it could learn your patterns?"
zh: 所以我说的是，“噢，等一下，如果它能学习你的模式会怎样？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was AI.
zh: 那就是 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so now let's put that together in a much cooler looking attractive package that costs five to six times more than the things that you're buying today.
zh: 所以现在让我们把它放在一个外观更酷、更具吸引力的包装里，价格要比你今天买的东西贵五到六倍。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that was the crazy opinion-based decision was, okay, yes, it's going to cost 249, but it's going to save you 800 to $1,200 a year.
zh: 但那是一个疯狂的主观决定，好吧，是的，它要花 249 美元，但它一年能帮你省 800 到 1200 美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it could pay for itself literally within a year or two.
zh: 所以它真的能在一年到两年内回本。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that was the big idea.
zh: 所以那就是大创意。

speaker: Unknown
en: And AI was brought to bear on that old problem and hopefully solve it in a new way.
zh: AI 被用来解决那个旧问题，并希望能以新的方式解决它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it starts with the pain, longtime pain, maybe habituated away pain that you have to discover and new technology bonded with that to then bring innovation in, revolution in, and then redefine the space in a way, which is what we did, which is not with just the product but how you installed it.
zh: 所以它始于痛点，长期的痛点，也许是你已经习惯了的痛点，你需要发现它，新技术与它结合，然后带来创新，带来革命，然后在某种程度上重新定义这个领域，这就是我们所做的，不仅仅是通过产品，还有你如何安装它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was always installed by third party installers as opposed to yourself, how you bought it, which was you bought it through the installer, you didn't buy it in Best Buy or somewhere else.
zh: 它总是由第三方安装人员安装，而不是你自己，你如何购买它，你是通过安装人员购买的，你不是在 Best Buy 或其他地方买的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we had to reinvent many different pieces of the puzzle to get Nest to be the Nest.
zh: 所以我们必须重新发明拼图中的许多不同部分，才能让 Nest 成为 Nest。

speaker: Unknown
en: It wasn't just the product.
zh: 不仅仅是产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was all the other things.
zh: 是所有其他的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like the iPod wasn't the iPod or the iPhone wasn't the iPhone.
zh: 就像 iPod 不仅仅是 iPod，或者 iPhone 不仅仅是 iPhone。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was the iPhone plus the app store, iPod plus iTunes and then the iTunes music store.
zh: 它是 iPhone 加上 App Store，iPod 加上 iTunes，然后是 iTunes 音乐商店。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have to think about the full thing you're trying to build, not just the one piece, even though that's what you might remember, you have to remember it's a system that you're going to innovate with.
zh: 所以你必须考虑你要构建的整个东西，不仅仅是一块，即使那可能是你记住的部分，你必须记住你要创新的是一个系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: So these two part formula you shared here, pain and new technologies.
zh: 所以你在这里分享的这个两部分公式，痛点和新技术的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The second part is really interesting.
zh: 第二部分真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Essentially it's what's the why now?
zh: 本质上就是，为什么是现在？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the new tech that has emerged that now allows us to solve this pain?
zh: 现在出现了什么新技术，让我们能够解决这个痛点？

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like that's a core part of it.
zh: 感觉那是其中的核心部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's because it's interesting because when you talked about the iPhone keyboard, it was a similar story of, okay, we can actually sort of do this virtual keyboard for the first time in history.
zh: 这很有趣，因为当你谈到 iPhone 键盘时，这是一个类似的故事，好吧，我们实际上可以在历史上第一次做这种虚拟键盘。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right, exactly.
zh: 对，确实。

speaker: Unknown
en: It could because of multi-touch.
zh: 它可以是因为多点触控。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's really what it was.
zh: 真的就是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we were just on the verge of having fast enough processors, and we were just on the verge.
zh: 而且我们刚刚拥有足够快的处理器，我们就在边缘上。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you look at the iPhone, or you're with Nest, iPhone or iPod, you can see where all these technologies were just coming to light.
zh: 如果你看看 iPhone，或者你用 Nest，iPhone 或 iPod，你可以看到所有这些技术都在刚刚浮出水面。

speaker: Unknown
en: So in the iPod, it was just now mass storage that was portable and battery operated.
zh: 所以在 iPod 里，就是现在的便携式和电池供电的海量存储。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was really what it was.
zh: 真的就是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was also MP3s or digital music.
zh: 而且也是 MP3 或数字音乐。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we also had high density.
zh: 而且我们还有高密度。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were the first products to have lithium ion or lithium prismatic polymer cells.
zh: 我们是拥有锂离子或锂棱柱聚合物电池的第一批产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it was new battery technology, new mass storage, portable mass storage and this new generation of digital music and we had ARM processors.
zh: 所以是新的电池技术，新的海量存储，便携式海量存储，以及这一代新的数字音乐，我们有 ARM 处理器。

speaker: Unknown
en: And really, really low power.
zh: 真的，非常低功耗。

speaker: Unknown
en: So all of those things had to come together to do that.
zh: 所以所有这些东西必须汇聚在一起才能做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then on the iPhone, it was multi-touch, but it wasn't just that.
zh: 然后在 iPhone 上，是多点触控，但也不仅仅是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was also the fact that now we had wifi everywhere.
zh: 事实上现在我们到处都有 WiFi。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we knew that 3G was coming because we was only 2.5 G when that was, and that was very slow, but we had wifi.
zh: 而且我们知道 3G 即将到来，因为那时还只是 2.5G，那非常慢，但我们有 WiFi。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had digital cameras.
zh: 而且我们有数码相机。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had the digital camera, we had digital video, which we had YouTube at the time.
zh: 我们有数码相机，我们有数码视频，我们那时有 YouTube。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it was all of those things just on the verge to say, this is what's going to be very different than what just came before it, like a Blackberry.
zh: 所以所有这些东西都在边缘上，以此来说明这将与刚刚出现在它之前的东西非常不同，比如 Blackberry。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which was really just a texting machine and nothing else.
zh: 那真的只是一个发短信的机器，没有别的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to close the loop on your advice on finding a great idea.
zh: 我想就你关于寻找好创意的建议做一个总结。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious about the flip side of when it's not good enough.
zh: 我好奇反面情况，什么时候创意还不够好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's like so many gadgets out there that are solving some pain level.
zh: 所以外面有那么多小玩意儿，它们在解决某种程度的痛点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe there's a new technology that they integrated, but it's not a big business.
zh: 也许它们整合了一项新技术，但这并不是一门大生意。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not a real company.
zh: 这不是一家真正的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the threshold?
zh: 阈值是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's a sign of, hey, this isn't big enough?
zh: 有什么迹象表明，嘿，这东西不够大？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, the iPod wasn't big enough.
zh: 嗯，iPod 就不够大。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took three generations of the iPod before it became successful.
zh: 它经历了三代 iPod 之后才获得成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, anyone who knows if they remember iPod before iPhone, because iPhone swallowed everything.
zh: 嗯，任何知道的人如果记得 iPhone 之前的 iPod，因为 iPhone 吞噬了一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the black hole of everything.
zh: 它是所有事物的黑洞。

speaker: Unknown
en: But iPod, the first generation was only successful with the Mac Geeks.
zh: 但是 iPod，第一代只对 Mac 极客来说是成功的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the Mac Geeks were less than 1% of the market.
zh: 而 Mac 极客只占市场的不到 1%。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the second generation was also that way.
zh: 然后第二代也是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'd sell everything we could for the most part in the first quarter and then it would die, because it was just the Mac aficionados, the loyalists who'd come and buy everything.
zh: 我们在很大程度上会在第一季度卖掉我们能卖的一切，然后它就会消亡，因为只是那些 Mac 爱好者，那些忠诚主义者会来买下一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it wasn't until the third generation where we made it work on Windows.
zh: 直到第三代我们才让它能在 Windows 上运行。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had the iTunes music store.
zh: 而且我们要有了 iTunes 音乐商店。

speaker: Unknown
en: Did it actually start to take off?
zh: 它真的开始腾飞了吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So sometimes you have to say, "We're on the right thing, but we need to make some changes to get this market going.
zh: 所以有时候你不得不说，“我们做对了东西，但我们需要做一些改变来推动这个市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was a real opinion-based decision.
zh: 那是一个真正的基于意见的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: The team that I was running, we were really clear that we had to have Windows connectivity out of the gate, or not out of the gate, but just after the first iPod shipped.
zh: 我领导的团队，我们非常清楚我们必须在发布时就具备 Windows 连接性，或者不是在发布时，而是在第一个 iPod 发货后紧接着。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Steve said, "Over my dead body, no way.
zh: 然后 Steve 说，“除非我死，没门。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is going to help us sell more Macs."
zh: 这将帮助我们卖出更多的 Mac。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we're like, "Okay, we're making the second one.
zh: 然后我们就像，“好的，我们在做第二个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, we're going to keep it with Mac only.
zh: 哦，我们要保持它仅限于 Mac。

speaker: Unknown
en: We just got to fix a few of these things and then all of a sudden it'll take off."
zh: 我们只需要修复其中的一些东西，然后突然间它就会腾飞。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was like, it didn't take off.
zh: 然后，它没有腾飞。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then it was a long story about how we finally got Windows connectivity, but there was always this skunk works thing project behind the scenes doing that.
zh: 所以这是一个很长的故事，关于我们最终如何获得 Windows 连接性的，但背后总是有一个像臭鼬工厂这样的项目在做那件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I did the same thing with Stylus.
zh: 我对 Stylus 做了同样的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Steve never wanted a stylist on the iPhone or the iPad.
zh: Steve 从不想要在 iPhone 或 iPad 上有一个 stylist。

speaker: Unknown
en: He never wanted it.
zh: 他从不想要它。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's like, "The finger is good enough.
zh: 他就像，“手指已经足够好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we can't do it with the finger."
zh: 而我们无法用手指做到这一点。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm like, "But we're going to have the B2B context and we're going to have form filling, and people are going to write."
zh: 我就像，“但我们将拥有 B2B 上下文，我们将有表单填写，人们将要书写。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's like, "I don't care."
zh: 他就像，“我不在乎。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That means we're going to get to Windows pen because he thought it was going to be like Windows pen, which was you had to use the pen for everything as opposed to using your finger.
zh: 这意味着我们将走向 Windows pen，因为他认为它会像 Windows pen 一样，就是你必须使用笔来做所有事情，而不是使用你的手指。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it was like, "Instead of pen dominant, I want it finger dominant."
zh: 所以就像，“我想要它是指为主导，而不是笔为主导。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's what he was saying.
zh: 但那就是他在说的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then when we added the stylus, which was another skunk works project, all of a sudden it came out and it was like, "Well, we had to have stylus."
zh: 但是当我们添加 stylus 时，这是另一个臭鼬工厂项目，突然间它出现了，就像，“嗯，我们必须有 stylus。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And now it's a big feature of the product.
zh: 现在它是产品的一个重要特性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not everyone uses it, but it is a big feature for certain professionals who really want that, and artists and hobbyists and stuff.
zh: 不是每个人都使用它，但对于真正想要它的某些专业人士，以及艺术家和爱好者之类的人来说，它是一个重要的特性。

speaker: Unknown
en: So sometimes you have to have those skunk works things that even if the opinion- based leader doesn't like it, you're like, "That seems like the right thing."
zh: 所以有时候你必须拥有那些臭鼬工厂的东西，即使基于意见的领导者不喜欢它，你也就像，“这似乎是正确的事情。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe not right now, but you can see it on the horizon so you just keep working on those things.
zh: 也许不是现在，但你能在地平线上看到它，所以你只要继续做这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we had to, like I said, the iPhone wasn't a hit right away.
zh: 但是我们必须，就像我说的，iPhone 并没有立即一炮而红。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was like a, "Oh yeah, it worked on AT&T and it was two and a half G and it was this and that."
zh: 它就像一个，“哦是的，它在 AT&T 上运行，它是两代半 G（2.5G），它是这和那。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It only worked in the US.
zh: 它只在美国运行。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we had to get multiple versions till we got out.
zh: 所以我们必须制作多个版本直到我们走出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's in my book, it's called, I think it's three generations.
zh: 那在我的书里，它被称为，我认为是三代。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything needs three generations.
zh: 一切都需要三代。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, Bill, there it is.
zh: 是的，Bill，就在那。

speaker: Unknown
en: Three generations.
zh: 三代。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've learned, you make the product, you fix the product, then you fix the business.
zh: 我学到了，你制造产品，你修复产品，然后你修复业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've never seen anyone get it all right the first time.
zh: 我从未见过有人第一次就把一切都做对。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would like to, but you get close.
zh: 你想一次搞定，但你只能接近目标。

speaker: Unknown
en: But make the product, fix the product after you get customer feedback and then make the business, which means make the margins.
zh: 但是要做出产品，在获得客户反馈后修正产品，然后再打造生意，也就是要做利润。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like on the first iPods, we weren't making any money.
zh: 比如在最初的几代 iPod 上，我们没赚任何钱。

speaker: Unknown
en: First iPhones, we weren't making any money.
zh: 第一代 iPhone，我们也没赚任何钱。

speaker: Unknown
en: The second iPhone and second iPod, okay, we started getting a little bit better numbers, but we got more or less the features dialed.
zh: 到了第二代 iPhone 和第二代 iPod，好吧，我们的数字开始稍微好看一点了，但我们或多或少把功能调整到位了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Third one was like, okay, Windows, we got the margins, we're getting up the volume, we got all the right [inaudible 00:31:48].
zh: 到第三代就像，好吧，Windows，我们有了利润，我们提升了销量，我们弄对了所有正确的 [inaudible 00:31:48]。

speaker: Unknown
en: And like go.
zh: 就像开始行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: Reliability, whatever else it had to be.
zh: 可靠性，以及它必须具备的其他任何东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you need to stick with your idea even if it's not necessarily going the first time, unless your brain-damaged about whatever it was, you have to restart.
zh: 所以即使你的想法第一次不一定行得通，你也必须坚持这个想法，除非你对那个东西脑子坏掉了，那你必须重新开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: But sometimes you have to hang in there.
zh: 但有时候你必须坚持下去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And same thing with Nest.
zh: Nest 也是一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had to hang in there with two generations of the smoke detector and a few generations of thermostat before we made the business work, not just make the product work.
zh: 我们在烟雾探测器的两代产品和恒温器的几代产品上不得不坚持下去，才让生意运作起来，而不仅仅是让产品运作起来。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so many directions I can go here.
zh: 我可以朝这个方向谈很多点。

speaker: Unknown
en: One that's interesting is how often Steve Jobs was actually wrong and how many times you had to [inaudible 00:32:24].
zh: 有趣的一点是 Steve Jobs 实际上错误的频率有多高，以及你不得不 [inaudible 00:32:24] 多少次。

speaker: Unknown
en: We all were wrong at certain times, but it's when you hit those, when you hit it big, when you get the right ones, they overshadow all the other stuff, but that's how you have to try and iterate.
zh: 我们在特定时刻都错了，但那是当你碰到那些时刻，当你大获成功，当你做对的时候，它们掩盖了所有其他东西，但这就是你必须尝试迭代的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jeff Bezos says the same thing.
zh: Jeff Bezos 也说了同样的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: I believe in the same thing.
zh: 我也相信同样的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: You got to fail a few times until you find your way, but you only fail if you stop.
zh: 你必须失败几次才能找到出路，但只有当你停止时才算失败。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you keep iterating and keep going, well then that's not failure.
zh: 如果你继续迭代并继续前进，那么那就不叫失败。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's called learning.
zh: 那叫学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other interesting part of the story you just shared here, it was one of my favorite stories in your book actually, the fact you implied this, but basically the first iPod was you had to have a Mac to use it.
zh: 你刚才分享的故事中另一个有趣的部分，实际上这是你书中我最喜欢的故事之一，你暗示了这一点，但基本上第一代 iPod 是你必须有一台 Mac 才能使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the idea is you want to sell Macs.
zh: 这个想法是你想卖 Mac。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a way to get people to buy Macs.
zh: 这是让人们购买 Mac 的一种方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way you tell it is basically this ended up saving Apple eventually, not selling Macs, but people buying iPods.
zh: 你讲述的方式基本上是这最终拯救了 Apple，不是靠卖 Mac，而是靠人们购买 iPod。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that became the big part of the business.
zh: 那成为了生意的主要部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The mantra was, "Steve, if we don't have Windows connectivity, the iPod doesn't cost $349.
zh: 信条是，“Steve，如果我们没有 Windows 连接性，iPod 就不是卖 349 美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: It costs $3,000 because you got to buy a Mac, and you got to move all your digital life over to it and everything else.
zh: 它要卖 3000 美元，因为你得买台 Mac，你得把你所有的数字生活迁移到它上面以及等等其他一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: People are not about to take a risk on this company that's almost bankrupt for $3,000.
zh: 人们不会冒险为一家几乎破产的公司花 3000 美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: So how are we going to do that?"
zh: 所以我们要怎么做？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, let's make sure it is only $349 and that they can try it.
zh: 好吧，我们要确保它只卖 349 美元，让他们能试用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And once they try the brand, they go, "Ooh, that's pretty interesting.
zh: 一旦他们试用了这个品牌，他们就会，“噢，那挺有意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe I should try other products from this company and give them a shot."
zh: 也许我应该试试这家公司的其他产品，给他们一个机会。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Because that was just such a sublime experience and that's how it all changed and that's how iPhone was able to be created, because without the iPod, I'm pretty sure there would've been no iPhone.
zh: 因为那是一种极其美妙的体验，一切都因此改变，iPhone 也就是这样能够被创造出来的，因为没有 iPod，我很确定就没有 iPhone。

speaker: Unknown
en: There would've probably been no Apple because it was close to bankruptcy.
zh: 可能也就没有 Apple 了，因为它离破产很近。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's crazy how close it was to that if some of these decisions went the wrong way.
zh: 如果当时这些决定有些走错了方向，当时离那个结果有多近，真是疯狂。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Very close.
zh: 非常近。

speaker: Unknown
en: Remember, there was no retail back then.
zh: 记住，那时候还没有零售。

speaker: Unknown
en: Apple retailers, none of that stuff.
zh: Apple 零售商，所有这些东西都没有。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people don't know how on the ropes Apple was back in 2001.
zh: 大多数人都不知道 2001 年的时候 Apple 有多么岌岌可危。

speaker: Unknown
en: So many stories, but I want to come back to the customer journey thread that you touched on, which I think is really, really important for people listening to this podcast to hear, something that you kind of reinforce again and again in the book.
zh: 故事太多了，但我想回到你提到的客户旅程这个线索，我认为对于听这个播客的人来说，这真的非常重要，你在书中一次又一次地强调这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have this awesome image that we're going to show of the entire customer journey.
zh: 你有一张我们要展示的很棒的图片，是关于整个客户旅程的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And your point that you make is so many product builders focus on building the product and think, build the best product we're going to win.
zh: 你提出的观点是，这么多产品制造者专注于制造产品，认为，构建最好的产品我们就会赢。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have this very important advice of there's so much more to it.
zh: 而你有一个非常重要的建议，就是除此之外还有更多东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Especially the marketing piece, talk about what you think builders don't really get, even though they hear this advice in many ways.
zh: 特别是营销部分，谈谈你认为制造者真正不理解的地方，尽管他们通过很多方式听到了这个建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: When we build products, we define products when we build them.
zh: 当我们制造产品时，我们在制造产品时定义产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have a good sense of the context when we are defining those things.
zh: 当我们定义这些东西时，我们对背景有很好的了解。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're living in that world.
zh: 我们就生活在那个世界里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we're like, "Oh, we're like this and these are our issues and this is who we are."
zh: 于是我们就说，噢，“我们就像这样，这些是我们的问题，这就是我们是谁。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And maybe you put on personas.
zh: 也许你会采用人设。

speaker: Unknown
en: We would strip out and figure out who our target customers were with their personas.
zh: 我们会剥离出来，弄清楚我们的目标客户是谁，连同他们的人设。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it a single mom with kids or is it a dual income, no kid family, or is it senior citizens or whatever?
zh: 是一位带孩子的单身母亲，还是双薪无子女家庭，或者是老年人，或者其他的什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And we would come up with these personas and we would live inside them as we're thinking about the product.
zh: 我们会构思出这些人设，然后在思考产品的时候就生活在这些人设里。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you have to also remember that these people, they live in the context, but they're not aware of your product in their context.
zh: 但你必须也要记住，这些人，他们生活在情境中，但在他们的情境中并没有意识到你的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you have to bring it home to them and meet them where they are.
zh: 所以你必须把它带给他们，在他们所在的地方与他们相遇。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so your marketing, your website, your Instagram ads, your whatever, your owned and earned and owned media that you do, you really need to put your product in their context and make the visuals and make the words and everything sing to them.
zh: 所以你的营销、你的网站、你的 Instagram 广告、你的任何东西、你做的自有媒体和赢得媒体，你真的需要把你的产品放进他们的情境中，让视觉效果和文字以及所有东西都对他们产生吸引力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if not, they're not going to get it.
zh: 如果不这样，他们就不会明白。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so too many times we just say," Well, if we just make the perfect product."
zh: 所以很多时候我们只是说，“好吧，只要我们做出完美的产品。”

speaker: Unknown
en: No, you have to put all for a consumer thing specifically, and it's also really good for a B2B product as well, is make sure you understand your customer.
zh: 不，你必须把所有这一切都放进去，特别是针对消费品，这对 B2B 产品也非常有益，就是要确保你了解你的客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you say the right word, they go, "Oh, they get me.
zh: 当你说出那个正确的词时，他们会说，“噢，他们懂我。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, I want to listen to these more."
zh: 噢，我想多听听这些。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is before they ever got the product.
zh: 而这是在他们真正得到产品之前。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to get them to convert in some way and they need to hear that you've already thought about their issues, or you know and you're living in their shoes.
zh: 你需要让他们以某种方式进行转化，而且他们需要听到你已经考虑过他们的问题，或者你知道并且你正设身处地为他们着想。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're like, "Yes, yes, yes.
zh: 他们就会说，“是的，是的，是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: More of that."
zh: 多来点这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Is this an emotional point?
zh: 这是一个情感点吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is this a rational point?
zh: 这是一个理性点吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're weaving this tapestry to get them to come to some kind of trial or purchase or conversion or something of the product.
zh: 你正在编织这张挂毯，让他们来到某种试用或购买或转化或产品的某个东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the best way to do that is word of mouth by other early adopters, but you got to do the same thing for the early... and I mean not the earliest early adopters, but the early adopters who other people trust to talk to.
zh: 最好的方法是其他早期采用者的口碑，但你也必须为早期的……做同样的事情，我的意思不是最早的早期采用者，而是其他人愿意信任并与之交谈的早期采用者。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because a lot of other people who are late adopters, they know, "Oh, that's wacky George, he tries everything and then [inaudible 00:37:20].
zh: 因为很多其他的晚期采用者，他们知道，“噢，那是古怪的乔治，他什么都试，然后 [听不清 00:37:20]。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, no, no."
zh: 不，不，不。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But if so-and-so's tried it, oh, I should take notice to that.
zh: 但是如果是某某人试过了，噢，我应该留意一下。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what you want to do is make sure you understand the gestation of your customer and where they are and where they are in the thing.
zh: 所以你想做的是确保你了解你客户的孕育过程以及他们在哪里以及他们在事情的哪个阶段。

speaker: Unknown
en: I also updated the crossing the chasm graphic in the book saying how we go about this in your product developments.
zh: 我还更新了书中的“跨越鸿沟”图表，说明了我们在你的产品开发中如何做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because you really have to think about who your target user set is and your target marketing based on the version of product you're in and how you're going to get those higher and higher volumes.
zh: 因为你真的必须考虑你的目标用户群是谁，以及基于你所处的产品版本的目标营销，以及你要如何获得那些越来越高的量。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it really means about speaking the context.
zh: 这真的意味着要说出情境。

speaker: Unknown
en: So maybe when you first ship, you can speak to those early adopters and you can put it in enough words that they're going to convert.
zh: 所以也许当你首次发布时，你可以对那些早期采用者说话，你可以用足够的词语来表达，让他们会转化。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then the second version of the product, you're getting these later adopters.
zh: 但随后产品的第二个版本，你获得了这些晚期采用者。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you've got to speak in their language.
zh: 你必须用他们的语言说话。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the third set of adopters who are really laggards, you've got to really speak to them and go through that and it could be totally different.
zh: 而第三组采用者，也就是真正的落后者，你必须真正地对他们说话，经历那个过程，这可能会完全不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this was a very interesting story that it was so emblazoned in my mind.
zh: 所以这是一个非常有趣的故事，它深深地铭刻在我的脑海里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Back in 2001, '2, '3, still when Apple had no presence outside of the US, Apple was really, it was selling in the US, some Canada, and some in Japan, but that was it and that was the max.
zh: 回到 2001、'2、'3 年，当时 Apple 在美国以外还没有任何业务，Apple 真的只是在美国销售，一些加拿大，和一些日本，仅此而已，那就是最大范围了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we were selling iPods into those areas.
zh: 所以我们在那些地区销售 iPod。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we had the early adapter, and then we had some other language.
zh: 于是我们有了早期适配器，然后我们有了某种其他的语言。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then by the third, fourth version of iPod, we had some really refined language for those late adopters.
zh: 然后到了 iPod 的第三、第四版，我们为那些晚期采用者准备了一些非常精细的语言。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then we said, "We're going to go back and get a push into Europe."
zh: 然后我们说，“我们要回去推动进入欧洲。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Because iPod hadn't taken off in Europe, so we're going to push iPod into Europe.
zh: 因为 iPod 在欧洲还没有起飞，所以我们要把 iPod 推向欧洲。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we're on, I think the fourth generation or something like that.
zh: 所以我们在，我想是第四代左右。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what did we do in Europe?
zh: 那么我们在欧洲做了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: We ran the same marketing as we were doing in the US in Europe.
zh: 我们在欧洲运行了和美国一样的营销。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the thing is it didn't resonate with the early adopters.
zh: 问题是它没有引起早期采用者的共鸣。

speaker: Unknown
en: It didn't resonate with the later adopters because we didn't have the same messages.
zh: 它也没有引起晚期采用者的共鸣，因为我们没有相同的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were going for a different set of people and we're like, ding, ding, ding, ding, ding.
zh: 我们针对的是不同的人群，我们就像，叮，叮，叮，叮，叮。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sales aren't working.
zh: 销售额不行。

speaker: Unknown
en: We got to change the marketing.
zh: 我们必须改变营销。

speaker: Unknown
en: We got to meet those Europeans where they're at, because they're slower to adopt technology.
zh: 我们必须接触那些欧洲人所在的地方，因为他们采用技术的速度较慢。

speaker: Unknown
en: Versus it starts with the coast and goes inward in the US.
zh: 相比之下在美国，它从沿海开始并向内陆延伸。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we're like, "Oh, we got to change things up."
zh: 所以我们就像，“噢，我们得改变一下。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so sometimes it's when you get to new areas, you have to rethink your marketing for that area and remember to meet them where they're at and where if it doesn't have an installed base for word of mouth, you're going to have to get that word of mouth started to work with those people to get that going.
zh: 所以有时候，当你进入新领域时，你必须重新思考该领域的营销，并记得在他们所在的地方与他们见面，以及如果它没有用于口碑的安装基础，你就必须让口碑开始运转，与这些人合作以实现这一目标。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it's different with all software products and all these things and many products can go global right away, but marketing, you still got to tell a story.

zh: 所以这提醒我，尽管我们可能压缩了采用拼图中某些部分的时间，因为我们可以分发得更快，但这并不意味着认知度可以加速。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that we can get people to intelligently understand with this product faster if we just say the words of somebody who might have known about the product for three years.
zh: 而且如果我们只是说出一个可能已经了解该产品三年的人的话，我们无法让人们更快地智能地理解这个产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: I got to ask about maybe the most famous, I don't know, tagline of all time when a product was launched, "1,000 songs in your pocket."
zh: 我必须询问一下也许是有史以来最著名的，我不知道，产品发布时的标语，“你口袋里的 1000 首歌。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Songs in your pocket.
zh: 口袋里的歌。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there a story behind that?
zh: 这背后有什么故事吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Back in the day at Apple, and maybe it's changed now, but back in the Steve days, there was the different functions of Apple.
zh: 回到在 Apple 的日子，也许现在变了，但在 Steve 的时代，Apple 有不同的职能。

speaker: Unknown
en: And because they were all lean because the company was only 4,000, 5,000 people and it was already doing Max and everything, each function was very, very functional.
zh: 因为它们都很精简，因为公司只有 4000、5000 人，而且已经在做 Max 和其他所有事情，每个职能都非常非常功能化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the marketing didn't get into the engineering and the design folks.
zh: 所以营销没有介入工程和设计人员。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was separate.
zh: 它是分开的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Steve was the hub and he was in each of them putting them together, like I said, the opinion-based decision maker.
zh: Steve 是枢纽，他在每个职能中，把它们组合在一起，就像我说的，基于意见的决策者。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when we heard the tagline for the first time, I was like, "That's genius."
zh: 所以当我们第一次听到那个标语时，我就像，“那是天才。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, how long that happened, what was the behind the scenes?
zh: 现在，那是多久以前的事了，幕后是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: We were just a tiny team.
zh: 我们只是一个小团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were getting this done in 10 months.
zh: 我们在 10 个月内完成了这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So everybody was just running for it.
zh: 所以每个人都在为此奔波。

speaker: Unknown
en: Versus iPhone, which was we had discussions of it, should we call it the iPod phone or should we call it the iPhone because Cisco had the iPhone back in the day, and there was copyright issues and trademark issues.
zh: 相比之下 iPhone，我们讨论过它，我们应该叫它 iPod phone 还是应该叫它 iPhone，因为 Cisco 在那个时代拥有 iPhone，并且存在版权问题和商标问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we were into that because it was also a two and a half year development, and we were in [inaudible 00:42:20].
zh: 所以我们深入参与了那件事，因为这也是为期两年半的开发，而且我们处于 [听不清 00:42:20]。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was betting the farm and we were going to cannibalize iPod.
zh: 这是把整个公司押上去，我们将要蚕食 iPod。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there was a lot more discussions, but in those early days of 1,000 songs, it was everybody running in literally it was really five to six months everything got done.
zh: 所以有更多的讨论，但在“1000 首歌”的早期日子里，真的是每个人都在奔跑，实际上真的就是五到六个月完成了一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because we really started it in April and it shipped in end of October.
zh: 因为我们真的在 4 月开始，在 10 月底发货。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think one of the core takeaways from what you shared here is even if you may have the perfect product, the marketing may be the gap that if something isn't working, it may-

en: And the thing is, I think we're starting to see that, the cracks of it might be more than cracks now, with OpenAI.

en: What is it?

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, oh, it's your answer machine, whatever you're like, "Well, what does it do for me?

en: And now I got to keep paying you.
zh: 现在我得一直付钱给你。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was fun as the demo, but what am I using it for every day?
zh: 作为 demo 很有趣，但我每天用它来做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And, oh, Claude, it does Claude code and it does code."
zh: 还有，噢，Claude，它做 Claude code 并且它做 code。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then what happens OpenAI is like, oh, "We have Codex now.
zh: 然后发生什么 OpenAI 就像，噢，“我们现在有 Codex。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then they were Sora, and they were this and they were that.
zh: 但后来他们是 Sora，他们是这个，他们是那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to do sex chat."
zh: 我们要做色情聊天。”

speaker: Unknown
en: What are you?
zh: 你是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's like, "Oh yeah, you're the great first Netscape.
zh: 所以就像，“噢，是啊，你是伟大的第一个 Netscape。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone went out and bought Netscape Explorer or whatever it was called, Netscape Navigator.
zh: 大家都出去买了 Netscape Explorer 或不管它叫什么，Netscape Navigator。

speaker: Unknown
en: Navigator.
zh: Navigator。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then all of a sudden it evaporated like, "Well, what do I use the net for?
zh: 然后突然间它蒸发了，就像，“好吧，我用网做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I got the tool to get onto it, but what do I use it for daily."
zh: 我有了上网的工具，但我每天用它做什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it had to develop.
zh: 它必须发展。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so OpenAI is now shifting like, "Oh, we got to get product teams and we got to start about product marketing."
zh: 所以 OpenAI 现在正在转变，就像，“噢，我们必须组建产品团队，我们必须开始关于产品营销。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So to me, it's marketing.
zh: 所以对我来说，这就是营销。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you are already thinking about the marketing, you're already going to start thinking about the product.
zh: 但是如果你已经在考虑营销，你就已经要开始考虑产品了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's the thing is when you're just thinking about the product, and it was really just a technology demo that went viral.

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're like, "Oh yeah."

speaker: Unknown
en: And they still keep winning on that.

speaker: Unknown
en: And now Anthropic's where they are and valued more and higher revenue and all that other stuff.

speaker: Unknown
en: So even if it's a software only product, yes, there's lots of hardware and servers and all the other stuff.
zh: 所以即使它只是一个软件产品，是的，有很多硬件、服务器和所有其他东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to think holistically and you need to think about the entire customer journey, the marketing, the sales pieces, the distribution pieces, the product definition piece, the messaging, the target markets, early on.
zh: 你需要整体思考，你需要考虑整个客户旅程、市场营销、销售环节、分销环节、产品定义环节、信息传递、目标市场，尽早考虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't leave it till later and and then back calculate it.
zh: 你不能把它留到以后再回过头来计算它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's why I say you should really make the press release before you more or less start the project.
zh: 这就是为什么我说你应该在项目差不多开始之前就真正把新闻稿做好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a whole chapter on that.
zh: 有一整章是关于这个的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Amazon's also very famous for that, this whole book on Working Backwards that goes really deep on this approach.
zh: 亚马逊也因此非常出名，这本《Working Backwards》的书深入探讨了这种方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the weird thing is it's working backwards.
zh: 但奇怪的是它是逆向工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: See, the thing saying working backwards, would a movie be created that way?
zh: 你看，说逆向工作，电影会那样创作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it called working backwards when you say, "I'm going to make a script, and I'm making the treatment, and I'm going to really know what it is.

zh: 我知道我的角色是谁，我如何进行角色发展」时，这叫逆向工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that really working backwards?
zh: 这真的是逆向工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: It sounds like it's backwards.
zh: 听起来像是倒着来的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's, no, that's the way you do it.
zh: 不，这才是你该做的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just because it's so technology led and technologist, and this is what I thought when I was 20, I was like, "Yeah, now it sounds backwards."

speaker: Unknown
en: It's actually, no, that's actually insane.

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not working backwards.

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just an insane way of working.

speaker: Unknown
en: Come on, let's really think through this.

speaker: Unknown
en: It says so much that you as one of the, I don't know, most successful insightful builders are so into marketing.

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's a really important takeaway for people, just how obsessed you are with that piece of building that I think a lot of builders don't think about at all.
zh: 我认为这真的是一个重要的收获，对人们来说，就是你对构建中的那部分有多么痴迷，我想很多构建者根本不会去思考那部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, when you live in that world and you live in the customer... Because you're coming from a customer point of view.
zh: 嗯，当你生活在那个世界里，生活在客户中……因为你是从客户的角度出发的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have to see the lens.
zh: 所以你必须通过那个镜头看。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the customer only sees what they see through the lens of marketing and sales.
zh: 客户只能看到他们通过市场营销和销售的镜头看到的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you have to be in their shoes and you go, "Okay, when I do the press release, I can only have three or four key features."
zh: 所以你必须站在他们的立场上，然后你会说，「好的，当我写新闻稿时，我只能有三个或四个关键特性。」

speaker: Unknown
en: After that, it becomes gobbledygook for a customer.
zh: 在那之后，对客户来说它就变成了天书。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you're like, "Okay, what are those three or four things?
zh: 所以你会想，「好的，那三四个东西是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, what is that?
zh: 好的，那是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's what we're going to focus on."
zh: 那就是我们要专注的东西。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And so no, we're not going to add five more features.
zh: 所以不，我们不打算再增加五个功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not going to make it sell better.
zh: 那不会让它卖得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or, "Oh no, we're going to cut these two features and ship it."
zh: 或者，「哦不，我们要删掉这两个功能并把它发布出去。」

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "Well, wait a second, we cut out two of our three key tentpole features.
zh: 就像是，「等等，我们删掉了我们三个关键支柱特性中的两个。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we going to sell that anymore?"
zh: 我们怎么还能把它卖出去？」

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's a holistic design.
zh: 所以这是一个整体设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not [inaudible 00:46:41].

speaker: Unknown
en: Because we think the technology's in service of the customer, not we're going to jam the technology down the customer's throat, and they're going to figure out how to use it.
zh: 因为我们认为技术是为客户服务的，而不是我们要把技术硬塞给客户，然后他们去想怎么使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's too much noise that you've got to make it frictionless, and you got to fit it in their world and see from their point of view go, "Oh, that's why I need it. "
zh: 噪音太多了，你必须让它无摩擦，你必须把它融入他们的世界，并从他们的角度看，「哦，这就是我需要它的原因。」

speaker: Unknown
en: General Magic was the perfect story for that.
zh: General Magic 是这方面完美的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know, your viewers should definitely watch the movie General Magic because-
zh: 我不知道，你的观众绝对应该看《General Magic》这部电影，因为——

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... we made the iPhone 15 years too early and that was a classic case where we were just making the things that were really cool, but nobody needed it.
zh: ……我们在 iPhone 出现的 15 年前就制造了它，这是一个典型的案例，我们只是在制作那些真的很酷的东西，但没有人需要它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that documentary's incredible.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, very different and probably people won't recognize me.
zh: 是的，非常不同，人们可能认不出我。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have a whole chapter about just not overworking in your career based on that experience.

speaker: Unknown
en: Which General Magic was for me.
zh: General Magic 对我来说就是那次经验。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to talk about something else around the evolution of the product management role and builders.
zh: 我想聊聊关于产品管理角色和构建者的演变的其他事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's really interesting, this is kind of going in a different direction, but it's all connected.
zh: 真正有趣的是，这有点是在往不同的方向发展，但这都是相互联系的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like you were so ahead on this idea that we are builders versus product managers, engineers, design.
zh: 我觉得你在「我们是构建者而不是产品经理、工程师、设计师」这个观点上非常超前。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have your book right here.
zh: 我这里有你的书。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's called Build.

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is what everyone's starting to call product managers, people on product teams.
zh: 这也是现在大家开始称呼产品经理、产品团队中的人的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious just what you're seeing with this kind of merging of these roles.
zh: 我只是好奇，你对这种角色的融合有什么看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you feel like everyone just becomes a builder and there's no more designer, engineer, product manager?

en: Do you think they'll continue being these functions, but more merged?
zh: 你认为它们会继续作为这些职能存在，但更加融合吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are you seeing with the discipline of product management specifically?

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, the discipline of product management sits between all of these functional roles, okay, whether that's marketing, sales, distribution, sometimes manufacturing, depends on what the product is, engineering obviously, and customer support.
zh: 嗯，产品管理学科位于所有这些职能角色之间，好的，无论是营销、销售、分发，有时是制造，取决于产品是什么，显然还有工程和客户支持。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when you sit between all of these things, maybe those roles shift or change, especially depending on what it is you're building and that kind of thing.

speaker: Unknown
en: But you have to interpret what's going on between all of them and stitch them all together to make this thing sing.

speaker: Unknown
en: And what we're saying is, "Oh, I can just, today in the AI world, I can just make a prompt and all of a sudden it gets spit out."
zh: 我们说的是，「噢，我可以，今天在 AI 世界里，我可以只是做一个提示然后突然它就被吐出来了。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And you don't know what all those little functions are.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know, maybe it was a month ago when the Claude source code leaked, right?

speaker: Unknown
en: Claude source code leaked.
zh: Claude 源代码泄露了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And everybody's like, "Oh my God, it leaked."
